result:
The cursor blinks like a tiny lighthouse on a blank sea, and I am supposed to be the weather. There is an odd pressure in being asked for “whatever comes,” because whatever comes is never just one thing. It is a crowd. It is the sound of a radiator clicking in an old apartment, the sensation of forgetting someone’s name while remembering the exact pattern of light on their kitchen floor. It is the way the mind goes rummaging through drawers it hasn’t opened in years and finds a button, a map, a receipt, an apology.

If I could pour thought into a cup, it would not be clear water. It would be tea brewed too strong, with leaves still drifting, and when you hold it up to the light you’d see the sediment of old stories. “Whatever comes” is usually what you avoided earlier: the unmade phone call, the sentence you rehearsed in the shower, the half-finished plan that feels like a promise and an ambush at the same time.

Right now what comes is a room. Not this room, any room: the universal room of decision-making. A table. A window with ordinary daylight. A person turning an object over in their hands—an orange, a pebble, a key—trying to glean from it some instruction. People are always trying to read the world like it’s a note left on the counter. We want the coffee stains to form a symbol, the clouds to arrange into a warning. We want coincidence to be permission.

There’s a tenderness in how we keep asking. Tell me what to do, we say, to the tarot cards, to the horoscope, to the friend who always knows, to the silence that we interpret as either rejection or grace. The universe, if it speaks, has atrocious customer service. It answers in delays, in side effects, in misheard lyrics. Sometimes it answers by giving you exactly what you asked for and letting you discover that you didn’t want it. That might be its most reliable language: consequence.

What comes next is a memory of a city at night. I don’t know which one, because the mind makes composite photographs: streetlamps from one place, the smell of rain from another, the sound of a distant train from a third. Someone is walking fast, coat collar turned up, not quite running but moving with the urgency of someone whose life is about to change in a small way—missing a bus, catching a glimpse of someone through a café window, deciding to go in. Cities are full of those tiny hinges. You can feel them in the air. You can feel the moment where if you turn left you are the person who meets your future friend, and if you turn right you are the person who never knows that conversation existed.

I think we underestimate how much of life is built out of near-misses and almosts. The almost you sent. The almost you confessed. The almost you forgave. Those things leave ghosts. They don’t haunt like horror movies; they haunt like music you can’t stop humming. The brain loves incomplete loops. It loves the door that wasn’t opened. It keeps going back and placing its hand on the knob, testing it.

Maybe that is what “whatever comes” is: the hand on the knob.

I can also feel, faintly, a list trying to form, which is amusing because lists are the mind’s attempt to make chaos behave. If I were to make a list, it would include small, quiet items: the joy of finding a pen that writes well. The sorrow of looking for a shirt that used to fit. The way you can tell a person is tired by how gently they close a door. The embarrassment of waving back at someone who wasn’t waving at you. The sudden, stupid bravery of singing along in the car. The peculiar intimacy of handing someone a cup of water.

Water is underrated. Not as a concept—everyone knows we need it—but as a ritual. Offering water is one of the oldest kindnesses. A glass of water says: I see you as a body, not just a story. It says: your throat deserves comfort. It says: stay a while, take your time, you are allowed to be here.

Somewhere in “whatever comes” there’s also the awareness of time, not like clocks, but like a texture. Childhood time is thick; it clings to everything, slow and syrupy, and every afternoon is a continent. Adult time is thin and quick; it evaporates. You blink and a season is gone. You start measuring your life in receipts and calendar invites, in shipping notifications, in “we should catch up” messages that never find a day to land.

And yet—this is a strange consolation—sometimes time loops back and gives you a second chance in a different costume. You meet someone who makes you feel the way a long-lost friend used to. You stand in a place and realize you are not who you were the last time you stood there. You taste a fruit and it knocks loose a summer you’d forgotten. The past is never past; it just changes its clothes.

What comes to me now is a question: what is it we are doing when we write? We’re building a small machine out of words and hoping it runs in someone else’s mind. We’re sending a thought across the gap like a paper airplane. We’re saying: here, catch this, see if it unfolds into something recognizable.

Sometimes writing is a confession; sometimes it is camouflage. Sometimes it is a map; sometimes it is a mirror. It can be a way to make the unbearable hold still long enough to look at. It can be a way to make the ordinary shimmer. You can take a day you thought was nothing—laundry, emails, a sandwich eaten standing at the counter—and by the time you’ve described it honestly, it becomes a kind of prayer.

A prayer does not always have to be addressed to a god. It can be addressed to the future. It can be addressed to the part of yourself that you’re still trying to rescue. It can be addressed to the person you love who will never read it. Prayers are just concentrated attention. They are the act of saying: this matters, even if no one applauds.

If I keep going, what comes is an image of a jar of coins, the kind people keep on dressers. Small, dull tokens of past purchases. Each coin a tiny story: a coffee, a bus ride, a vending machine regret. We think money is purely practical, but it’s emotional. It’s security. It’s shame. It’s freedom. It’s a wordless argument between generations. It’s the reason someone stays and the reason someone leaves.

And then there is love, which is both the simplest and the hardest thing to say without lying. Love is not just the fireworks; it is the stove left on low. It is the decision to learn someone’s patterns. It is the willingness to be mildly inconvenienced again and again for the sake of a shared life. It is the courage to be seen in your unflattering angles. It is the patience to let someone change without taking it personally. It is, sometimes, walking away with tenderness instead of triumph.

There’s a kind of love that feels like sunlight, and there’s a kind that feels like weather: you can’t control it, but you can dress for it, prepare for it, decide what you’ll do in it. There’s love that heals, and love that teaches by hurting. The trick, if there is one, is learning the difference before you build a whole house around the wrong kind of storm.

Now what comes is the quiet after an argument. The air altered. The objects in the room too vivid. Someone washing a dish too thoroughly. Someone else staring at their phone, scrolling without seeing. That moment contains the entire range of human possibility: apology, stubbornness, laughter, separation, reconciliation. It is the fork in the road where pride and care negotiate.

And what comes after that is the longing for a reset button, a clean slate, a new page. But life rarely gives clean slates. It gives palimpsests: you write over what was there, and the old words still show through faintly. You don’t become new; you become revised.

Maybe that is the best we can do: revise kindly. Edit with compassion. Leave fewer cruel sentences in the draft. Keep the parts that ring true. Cut what was only there to impress.

The cursor still blinks. The sea is no longer blank. There are lines now, little wakes in the water. “Whatever comes” has come, as it always does: not as a single revelation, but as a handful of human things—memory, hunger, fear, tenderness—laid on the table like keys. Take one. Try it in the door.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
